title: DR Strategies and RTO/RPO Trade-offs
description:
## Near-zero downtime, near-zero data loss

A payments platform must survive the loss of an entire region with **near-zero RTO and near-zero RPO** — essentially no downtime and effectively no lost transactions — and the business accepts a high cost to achieve it. Which disaster-recovery strategy fits, and why?

options.text: Active-active multi-region (multi-site): full production capacity runs in two or more regions simultaneously serving live traffic with continuous data replication, so a region loss is absorbed with near-zero RTO/RPO. It is the most expensive option because you fully run (and keep in sync) more than one region. RTO is how long recovery takes (max tolerable downtime); RPO is the maximum acceptable data loss measured as a point in time before the incident.
Backup and restore: keep regular backups in another region and rebuild on demand. With frequent enough backups this achieves near-zero RTO/RPO at the lowest cost.
Pilot light: keep only a minimal core (e.g. a replicated database) running in the second region and provision the rest after failover. This gives near-zero RTO because the database is already warm.

Warm standby: run a scaled-down but always-on copy of the full stack and scale it up on failover. Because it is always running, warm standby delivers identical RTO/RPO to active-active at lower cost, making active-active never worthwhile.

explanation: The spectrum, from cheapest/slowest to most expensive/fastest, is: **backup-and-restore** (recreate from backups — hours of RTO, RPO bounded by backup frequency), **pilot light** (core data replicated, minimal compute running, the rest provisioned on failover — RTO in tens of minutes), **warm standby** (a scaled-down but always-running full stack that you scale up — RTO in minutes), and **active-active / multi-site** (full capacity live in multiple regions with continuous replication — **near-zero RTO and RPO**, highest cost). Only active-active meets *near-zero on both* RTO and RPO, so (a) is correct. **Backup-restore (b)** always incurs restore/rebuild time and loses everything since the last backup — it cannot give near-zero RTO/RPO and is the cheapest precisely because of that trade-off. **Pilot light (c)** still has to provision and scale the non-core tiers after failover, so RTO is not near-zero even though the DB is warm. **Warm standby (d)** is faster than pilot light but still requires scaling up and cutover, so it does not match active-active's near-zero RTO, and the blanket claim that it makes active-active "never worthwhile" is false. Definitions: **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = the maximum tolerable time to restore service after an incident; **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = the maximum tolerable amount of data loss, expressed as the point in time before the incident to which you can recover.

hints.title: RTO vs RPO
hints.body: RTO = how long you can be down (time to recover). RPO = how much data you can lose (the recovery point before the incident). Near-zero on both is the most demanding target.

hints.title: Cost tracks recovery speed
hints.body: backup-restore < pilot light < warm standby < active-active in both cost and recovery speed. Only active-active runs full capacity everywhere at once, giving near-zero RTO/RPO.
